selection.greatFrontendReasonIfOmitted: I did not confirm a GreatFrontEnd page directly focused on static versus dynamic typing in JavaScript. Nearby interview pages cover coercion or specific operators, but not this exact type-system comparison.

competitors.label: MDN Data types and data structures
competitors.selectionReason: MDN is the canonical baseline for JavaScript's type system, values, primitives, objects, and runtime behavior.
theirStrengths: It is the authoritative language reference for JavaScript types and values.
theirGaps: It is broader than the exact static-versus-dynamic-typing interview question and less focused on runtime validation trade-offs.

realWorldUseCases: An analytics pipeline ingests mixed payloads from browser events.
MDN explains the JavaScript type system accurately, but our page ties the topic more directly to mixed payloads and runtime validation failures.
actionableExamples: Our page now includes concrete coercion and runtime-guard examples, while the MDN page is more of a reference overview.
followUpConfusion: MDN remains strong on the full type-model background, so the broader follow-up comparison stays close.
Add one short note on union-style runtime narrowing or schema validation if we later want a clearer docs-level follow-up edge.

competitors.winReason: MDN is the broad language reference, but our page is stronger for this interview question because it turns dynamic typing into concrete runtime validation and payload-shape trade-offs.

competitors.label: Data types and structures
competitors.selectionReason: web.dev is a strong tutorial-style competitor for JavaScript types and runtime behavior, even though it is broader than the static-vs-dynamic framing.
theirStrengths: It is a practical learning resource that explains JavaScript values and runtime type behavior clearly.
theirGaps: It is less directly framed as the static-versus-dynamic typing interview contrast and lighter on the TypeScript comparison.

realWorldUseCases: An analytics pipeline ingests mixed payloads from browser events.
followUpConfusion: JavaScript is dynamically typed, not statically typed.

realWorldUseCases: web.dev is practical on data types, but our page is more directly tied to runtime data-shape drift and developer workflow trade-offs.
actionableExamples: Its examples are still practical enough that the actionable-example criterion stays close.
followUpConfusion: Our page handles the likely follow-up more directly by contrasting JavaScript with TypeScript and explaining why runtime validation still matters.

actionableExamples: If needed later, add one shorter before-and-after TypeScript-versus-runtime-validation example.

competitors.winReason: web.dev is a good general types tutorial, but our page is better for this exact question because it keeps the answer centered on dynamic typing, weak typing, and the TypeScript follow-up.

competitors.label: JavaScript - How is JS Dynamically Typed?
competitors.selectionReason: This is a direct tutorial-style competitor for the exact dynamic-typing search intent.
theirStrengths: It appears directly aligned with the dynamic-typing question intent.
theirGaps: It is likely more definitional than our runtime-validation and TypeScript workflow framing.

realWorldUseCases: An analytics pipeline ingests mixed payloads from browser events.
GeeksforGeeks is direct on the concept, but our page gives a stronger product-facing reason dynamic typing matters in frontend systems.
actionableExamples: A direct tutorial page still offers concrete examples, so the example criterion remains close.
followUpConfusion: Our page goes further on the likely next questions: weak typing, coercion, and why TypeScript does not remove runtime validation.

actionableExamples: Keep the parsePrice runtime-guard example near the JavaScript-versus-TypeScript block so the page stays practical.

competitors.winReason: This tutorial is direct on dynamic typing, but our page is more complete for interview use because it connects the concept to coercion, TypeScript workflow, and runtime validation.
